caseName: Cullen v The State of New South Wales
oneLine: Police owe bystanders a duty of care when they move into a crowd to make an arrest — but a proportionate response to a real threat is not negligence, even when someone gets hurt.
whatHappened: On 26 January 2017, an “Invasion Day” protest march of about 5,000 people moved through inner Sydney. Laura CULLEN was watching from the crowd. As officers of the Operational Support Group moved in — and while Leading Senior Constable Livermore was arresting a marcher — a melee erupted and CULLEN was knocked to the ground and seriously injured. At trial she was awarded $800,000; the NSW Court of Appeal reversed that. She appealed to the High Court.
whatHeld: The appeal turned on three questions — duty, breach, and scope. Duty — yes: when intervening in the march, the officers owed a duty to avoid physical injury to a class of people that included bystanders foreseeably at risk from the operation. Breach — no: their actions were not unreasonable given the public-safety threat they were responding to at the time. With no breach, the scope question did not arise. Appeal dismissed.
whatItMeans: Read it for the principle, not the charge — this is NSW civil negligence, not WA criminal law, so it changes no offence you lay. But the principle is High Court and it travels: when you intervene operationally (a crowd, a public-order job, an arrest in a tight space) you owe bystanders a duty of care, and you are judged on the reasonableness of your response to the threat as it appeared at the time, not with hindsight. What saved the officers was a documented, proportionate plan (their “Operational Orders” and “Actions On”) — so record your threat assessment and why the response was proportionate (running sheet, notes, BWC). The same duty/breach reasoning runs through the Civil Liability Act 2002 (WA).
verdict: Useful for use-of-force and civil-liability exposure. It does not change how you investigate, gather evidence, or lay a charge.

court: Supreme Court of WA — Court of Appeal
caseName: Gibson v The State of Western Australia
weight: WA Court of Appeal — binds WA courts below
oneLine: On the objective facts, police had enough to reasonably suspect GIBSON — he should have been treated as a suspect with full rights, not interviewed as a witness. The bar for reasonable suspicion is low.
whatHappened: Arising from the killing of Joshua WARNEKE at Broome, GIBSON was spoken to as a witness under s 28 of the CIA when, on the objective facts known to police, he should have been treated as a suspect. The Court of Appeal found the failure to afford him a suspect’s rights (ss 137–138 CIA) produced a miscarriage and quashed the conviction.
whatHeld: Reasonable suspicion is judged objectively — the subjective facts an officer holds, assessed against what would be reasonable to a reasonable person. On these facts that threshold was met, so GIBSON should have had suspect status and the accompanying rights. The bar is low, but status must be decided on the objective facts at the time.
whatItMeans: Your go-to authority on reasonable suspicion (s 4 CIA), which underpins every CIA power. Decide a person’s status before you ask the first question — the instant the objective facts cross the line, caution and give full rights, or you lose what follows. Build the habit: every time you use s 4 (door, arrest, search), take two minutes and record this was my suspicion at the time — date, time, the facts, before you act.
verdict: Directly changes how you work: status discipline and a contemporaneous s 4 note on every job.

outcome: Leading authority on lies as consciousness of guilt (the Edwards direction)
weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: A lie shows consciousness of guilt only if it is deliberate, material, and told because the truth would implicate — otherwise it just goes to credit.
whatHappened: The High Court’s statement of when an accused’s lie can be used as evidence of guilt rather than merely as a matter of credit. (Held at principle level here — facts not reproduced.)
whatHeld: A lie can be evidence of a consciousness of guilt only where it is (1) deliberate, (2) material — about a circumstance connected to the offence, and (3) told because the truth would implicate the accused. A lie failing those limbs goes only to credit, and the jury must be directed carefully so ordinary lies aren’t treated as proof of guilt.
whatItMeans: When a suspect gives a false account it can become evidence, not just bad credit — but only if it meets the three limbs. So lock the account down in free recall before you challenge: pin them to a clear, detailed version first, so a later lie is demonstrably deliberate and material. This is the rule tied to the SEPTAGON statement in the lecture.
verdict: Changes interview craft: secure the account before the challenge so a lie can carry evidential weight.

weight: High Court — binds all Australian courts
oneLine: The High Court’s worked example of lies and conduct as consciousness of guilt in a circumstantial murder case — and of drawing inferences properly. It restored the murder verdict.
whatHappened: Gerard BADEN-CLAY was convicted of murdering his wife, Allison. The case was circumstantial; his conduct and lies — including an affair and a claim that scratches on his neck were ‘from shaving’ — featured at trial. The Queensland Court of Appeal substituted manslaughter; the High Court restored the murder conviction, addressing how inferences may be drawn in a circumstantial case.
whatHeld: Lies and conduct can support an inference of consciousness of guilt in a circumstantial case, and an appellate court must respect the jury’s role in drawing the available inferences. The murder verdict was restored.
whatItMeans: The best worked example for understanding Edwards lies — relied on precisely because it ran right through the High Court. For your circumstantial cases it’s the authority on how lies/conduct and inference-drawing hold together; pair it with Edwards when you build the brief.
verdict: Know it as the High Court authority you rely on for lies and inference in circumstantial cases.

court: Supreme Court of WA — Court of Appeal
outcome: Voluntary intoxication cannot ground unsoundness of mind (ss 27/28 Criminal Code)
weight: WA Court of Appeal — binds WA courts below
oneLine: Voluntary or intentional intoxication cannot ground unsoundness of mind under ss 27/28 of the Criminal Code — the Code is read on its own terms.
whatHappened: The appeal concerned the relationship between intoxication and the mental-impairment defence under the WA Criminal Code. (The instructor placed the facts at a Greenwood murder — confirm the facts on JADE; the legal point and citation are verified.)
whatHeld: Voluntary/intentional intoxication cannot found unsoundness of mind under ss 27/28 of the Criminal Code; the Code’s mental-state provisions are construed on their own terms, not by the old common-law learning on drunkenness.
whatItMeans: This is why you get bloods early. For any serious matter (murder, sexual offence, GBH), arrange a forensic procedure for blood ASAP — before alcohol/meth/GHB metabolises. If the defence runs mental impairment, proving a voluntarily-taken intoxicant in the system takes the insanity route away. As the DSS put it: bloods, bloods, bloods.
verdict: Changes an investigative step: forensic bloods as early as possible to defeat the intoxication/insanity defence.
